old_claim.note: Algorithms & Computation Theory provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency remains because the scoped technology is a VLSI RISC microprocessor implementation; compiler behavior and instruction-set design matter, but broad algorithms/computation theory is too generic as a direct edge.

source_shape.source_locator: IEEE ETHW describes Berkeley RISC-I around simplified instructions, VLSI processor design, registers, pipelined execution, C programs, and Unix, but not broad algorithms theory as a direct prerequisite.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source supports compiler-aware processor design rather than a generic algorithms/computation-theory dependency.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports removing the broad edge because the graph lacks a source-checked compiler-specific prerequisite here, and algorithms_computation_theory overstates the direct causal boundary.

ontology_before: The graph used broad algorithms/computation theory as a direct RISC prerequisite.
ontology_after: The graph models RISC microprocessors through microprocessor lineage and VLSI implementation rather than generic theory.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: direct risc_microprocessors -> algorithms_computation_theory edge removed.
would_reject_if: The node were rescoped to RISC compiler research rather than RISC microprocessor implementation.
A stronger source showed broad algorithms/computation theory was a direct prerequisite for Berkeley RISC-I rather than background computer science.
short_reason: Algorithms theory is too broad for the scoped RISC microprocessor implementation.
